oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Memorial to Jean Pattison the African Queen
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Sullivan, Robin
Allen, Melanie
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 51 Number 1 (January/February 2024).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.The African Queen ~ Jean Pattison Aviculture Loses a Legendby Robin Sullivan and Melanie Allen with Rick Jordan How do you memorialize a legend? Ask anyone in the avicultural world if they knew Jean Pattison and a smile usually materializes on their face and they have a story to tell you about Jean, “The African Queen ”. The stories are usually heartfelt tales of how at one time or another Jean helped them with a problem or a challenge with their birds. Even if you didn ’t know Jean, you immediately noticed her grey hair with a patch of bright candy -apple -red along the back and down her neck, imitating the plumage of her favorite birds: African Grey Parrots.Jean was a one-of-a-kind parrot breeder, lover, and enthusiast. None of her lectures on the biology of African parrots were boring. In fact, her antics, anecdotes, and true -life stories were exciting as well as educational. She always shared what she knew and sought out the latest information from biologists and other breeders alike; she was the true definition of an aviculturist.A memorial to Jean should first include her many accomplishments in aviculture. Her official biography reads like a who ’s who in aviculture with so many successes one began to wonder if it was about only one person. This is a snippet of her story in the bird world. In 1984 she acquired a pair of Senegal Parrots, from there her fascination with African parrots grew to include the African Grey, and then naturally more Poicephalus. Her studied observation of the Senegal Parrot taught her about behavior and social patterns that she was able to apply to other African parrotsClick the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 51 No. 1 (2024): Watchbird January/February 2024; 6-7
